subject: Physics
q:
Match List I and List II

  List I   List II
A Microwaves I Physiotherapy
B UV rays II Treatment of cancer
C Infra-red rays III Lasik eye surgery
D X-rays IV Aircraft navigation

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
Match List I and List II

  List I   List II
A Microwaves I Physiotherapy
B UV rays II Treatment of cancer
C Infra-red rays III Lasik eye surgery
D X-rays IV Aircraft navigation

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
A. Microwave → IV

Microwaves have short wavelength hence low energy dispersion during long distance communication. Thus, they are used in radar system.
B. UV rays → III

LASIK uses an excimer laser (an ultraviolet laser) to remove a thin layer of corneal tissue.

C. Infra-red →I

Infrared radiation in the device called infrared lamp is used in physiotherapy. This light is useful in reducing pain, decreasing inflammation, and speeding up wound care and tissue repair.


D. X-ray → II

Radiation therapy uses high-energy particles or waves, such as x-rays to destroy or damage cancer cells.

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> propagates in free space along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. At a particular space and time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo> </mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at this point?
question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> propagates in free space along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. At a particular space and time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mover><mrow><mi>j</mi><mo> </mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at this point?
solution:
It is given that 

 E→=6.6j^( V m-1).

The relation between the electric, magnetic field and speed of light is E→×B→=c→.

Clearly,   B→=B0(k^)

Using the relation,
     cB0=E
⇒B0=Ec=6.63×108=2.2×10-8 T

subject: Physics
q: A radio transmitter transmits at $830 \mathrm{kHz}$. At a certain distance from the transmitter magnetic field has amplitude $4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$. The electric field and the wavelength are respectively
question: A radio transmitter transmits at $830 \mathrm{kHz}$. At a certain distance from the transmitter magnetic field has amplitude $4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$. The electric field and the wavelength are respectively
solution:
Frequency of EM wave $v=830 \mathrm{KHz}$ $=830 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$.
Magnetic field, $B=4.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~T}$
As we know, frequency, $v=\frac{c}{\lambda}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { or } \lambda & =\frac{c}{v}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{830 \times 10^3} \\
\lambda & \simeq 360 \mathrm{~m} \\
\text { And, } E & =B C=4.82 \times 10^{-11} \times 3 \times 10^8 \\
& =0.014 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Due to presence of an em-wave whose electric component is given by $\mathrm{E}=100 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx}) \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$, a cylinder of length 200 cm holds certain amount of em-energy inside it. If another cylinder of same length but half diameter than previous one holds same amount of em-energy, the magnitude of the electric field of the corresponding em-wave should be modified as
question: Due to presence of an em-wave whose electric component is given by $\mathrm{E}=100 \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{kx}) \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$, a cylinder of length 200 cm holds certain amount of em-energy inside it. If another cylinder of same length but half diameter than previous one holds same amount of em-energy, the magnitude of the electric field of the corresponding em-wave should be modified as
solution:
Energy density of an $E M_{\text {wave }}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon E_0^2$, whare $E_0$ is the amplitude of the wave.
Since total energy is same for both cylinders
$\begin{aligned}
& \left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon E_1^2\right) \pi R_1^2 L_1=\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon E_2^2\right) \pi R_2^2 L_2 \\
& \Rightarrow E_1^2 R_1^2 L_1=E_2^2 R_2^2 L_2 \\
& \text { or } E_2=\frac{E_1 R_1}{R_2} \sqrt{\frac{L_1}{L_2}}=\frac{100 \mathrm{~d}}{(\mathrm{~d} / 2)} \sqrt{\frac{L_1}{L 1}}=200 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C} \\
& \qquad \quad\left[\because L_1=L_2=200 \mathrm{~cm}\right]
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow$ The amplitude of corresponding $E M$ wave is 200 N/C
or the wave is $E=200 \sin (\omega t-k x) \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$

subject: Physics
q:
A plane electromagnetic wave travels in a medium of relative permeability 1.61 and relative permittivity 6.44. If magnitude of magnetic intensity is 4.5×10-2 A m-1 at a point, what will be the approximate magnitude of electric field intensity at that point ?

(Given : Permeability of free space μ0=4π×10-7 N A-2, speed of light in vacuum c=3×108 m s-1)
question:
A plane electromagnetic wave travels in a medium of relative permeability 1.61 and relative permittivity 6.44. If magnitude of magnetic intensity is 4.5×10-2 A m-1 at a point, what will be the approximate magnitude of electric field intensity at that point ?

(Given : Permeability of free space μ0=4π×10-7 N A-2, speed of light in vacuum c=3×108 m s-1)
solution:
The speed of light in a given medium is given by,

v=cμrεr

Magnetic field is B=μrμ0H.

Also, the relation between the magnetic field and electric field is given by,

E=vB⇒E=cμrεr×μrμ0H=cεr×μr×μ0H⇒E=3×1086.44×1.61×4π×10-7×4.5×10-2=8.48 V m-1

subject: Physics
q: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\hat{i} 40 \cos \omega(\mathrm{t}-z / \mathrm{c}) \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The magnetic field induction of this wave is (in SI unit) :
question: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\hat{i} 40 \cos \omega(\mathrm{t}-z / \mathrm{c}) \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The magnetic field induction of this wave is (in SI unit) :
solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}} 40 \cos \omega\left(\mathrm{t}-\frac{\mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{c}}\right)$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$ is along $+\mathrm{x}$ direction
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ is along $+\mathrm{z}$ direction
So direction of $\vec{B}$ will be along +y and magnitude of $B$ will be $\frac{E}{c}$
So answer is $\frac{40}{c} \cos \omega\left(t-\frac{z}{c}\right) \hat{j}$

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I (Wavelength range of electromagnetic spectrum) with List II (Method of production of these waves) and select the correct option from the options given below the lists.
  List I   List II
(a) 700 nm to 1 mm (i) Vibration of atoms and molecules.
(b) 1 nm to 400 nm (ii) Inner shell electrons in atoms moving from one energy level to a lower level.
(c) < 10-3 nm (iii) Radioactive decay of the nucleus.
(d) 1 mm to 0.1 m (iv) Magnetron valve.
question:
Match List I (Wavelength range of electromagnetic spectrum) with List II (Method of production of these waves) and select the correct option from the options given below the lists.
  List I   List II
(a) 700 nm to 1 mm (i) Vibration of atoms and molecules.
(b) 1 nm to 400 nm (ii) Inner shell electrons in atoms moving from one energy level to a lower level.
(c) < 10-3 nm (iii) Radioactive decay of the nucleus.
(d) 1 mm to 0.1 m (iv) Magnetron valve.
solution:
We know, the range of wavelengths between 700 nm to 1 mm, is referred as Infrared rays. These are produced by heating an object to such an extent that the molecules start vibrating and it radiates infrared waves. When the object is not hot enough such that it can emit visible light, therefore, it radiates Infrared waves.
Thus, option (a) in list I matches with Option (i) in list II.


The range of wavelengths between 1 nm to 400 nm, is referred to as ultraviolet rays. Ultraviolet rays are produced due to atomic excitation in a gaseous discharge tube. This atomic excitation occurs when an atom from one energy level jumps to a lower energy level.
Thus, option (b) in list I matches with Option (ii) in list II.

The range of wavelengths <10-3 nm are known as the Gamma rays. Some of the radioactive substances, when decay releases a spectrum, which falls in the range of <10-3 nm, they emit electromagnetic waves called the gamma rays.
Thus, we can say, Thus, option (c) in list I matches with Option (iii) in list II.

We know again, the range of wavelength 1 mm to 0.1 m, is referred to as microwaves, and they are produced inside an oven in a tube like structure, called the magnetron.

Thus, we can say, option (d) in list I matches with Option (iv) in list II.

subject: Physics
q: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow><mi>c</mi></mfrac></mfenced><msup><mi>NC</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the intensity of the wave if it is propagating along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis in the free space. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow><mi>c</mi></mfrac></mfenced><msup><mi>NC</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Find the intensity of the wave if it is propagating along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis in the free space. (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Given, E=56.5sinωt-xc N C-1

Comparing it with the general equation of electromagnetic wave, 

E=E0sin ωt-kx

We have, E0=56.5 N C-1

So, the intensity will be  I=12ε0E02c

=12×8.85×10-12×56.52×3×108

=4.24  W m-2

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Electromagnetic waves carry energy as they travel through space and this energy is equally shared by the electric and magnetic fields.

Statement II: When electromagnetic waves strike a surface, a pressure is exerted on the surface.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Electromagnetic waves carry energy as they travel through space and this energy is equally shared by the electric and magnetic fields.

Statement II: When electromagnetic waves strike a surface, a pressure is exerted on the surface.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
solution:
Electromagnetic wave carries energy shared equally in magnetic and electric field which is given by, =12ε0E2=B22μ0, as E=cB and c2=1μ0ε0.

Also, Electromagnetic wave carries momentum and therefore exerts force when it strikes a surface.

subject: Physics
q:
For a plane electromagnetic wave, the magnetic field at a point x and time t is :

B→x, t=1.2×10−7sin0.5×103x+1.5×1011tk^ T.

The instantaneous electric field E→ corresponding to B→ is :
question:
For a plane electromagnetic wave, the magnetic field at a point x and time t is :

B→x, t=1.2×10−7sin0.5×103x+1.5×1011tk^ T.

The instantaneous electric field E→ corresponding to B→ is :
solution:
E0=B0×C=1.2×10-7×3×108=36

As the light is propagating in x direction.

& E^×B^||C^

∴ E→ should be in j^ direction.

So electric field E→=E0sinE→·(x,t)=-36sin0.5×103x+1.5×1011tj^Vm

subject: Physics
q: For an electromagnetic wave propagating through vacuum, $\vec{k}$, $\vec{E}$ and $\omega$ represent propagation vector, electric field and angular frequency, respectively. The magnetic field associated with this wave is represented by :
question: For an electromagnetic wave propagating through vacuum, $\vec{k}$, $\vec{E}$ and $\omega$ represent propagation vector, electric field and angular frequency, respectively. The magnetic field associated with this wave is represented by :
solution:
For an electromagnetic wave, the direction of wave propagation $\hat{k}$ is along the direction of $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$.

Since $\vec{E}$, $\vec{B}$, and $\vec{k}$ form a mutually perpendicular right-handed system, the direction of the magnetic field is given by $\hat{B} = \hat{k} \times \hat{E}$.

The magnitudes of the electric and magnetic fields are related by $B = \dfrac{E}{c}$.

Using the relation for the speed of light $c = \dfrac{\omega}{k}$, we get $B = \dfrac{kE}{\omega}$.

Combining the magnitude and direction gives:

$\vec{B} = B\hat{B} = \dfrac{kE}{\omega} (\hat{k} \times \hat{E})$

Since $\vec{k} = k\hat{k}$ and $\vec{E} = E\hat{E}$, this simplifies to:

$\vec{B} = \dfrac{\vec{k} \times \vec{E}}{\omega}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\vec{k} \times \vec{E}}{\omega}$

question: For plane electromagnetic waves propagating in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi></math>-direction, which one of the following combinations gives the correct possible direction for <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> field respectively?
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
The direction of propagation of the electromagnetic waves is along E→×B→.

Also E→·B→=0

When E→ is along -2 i^-3 j^

And B→ is along 3 i^-2 j^

Then E→×B→=-2i^-3j^×3i^-2j^

=4 k^+9 k^

=13 k^ is along +ve direction also E→·B→=0

q: The magnetic field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The amplitude of the electric field would be
question: The magnetic field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>11</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The amplitude of the electric field would be
solution:
The ratio of electric field amplitude to the magnetic field amplitude of an electromagnetic wave is equal to the speed of light in the medium. Therefore,

c=E0B0⇒E0=cB0

⇒E0=3×108×2×10-8

⇒E0=6 V m-1

According to the given equation B→=2×10-8sin0.5×103x+1.5×1011tj^ T the wave travels in x direction. As electric field and magnetic field vibrate perpendicular to the velocity of light, electric field can not be along x axis.

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave of intensity $50 \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ enters in a medium of refractive index 'n' without any loss. The ratio of the magnitudes of electric fields, and the ratio of the magnitudes of magnetic fields of the wave before and after entering into the medium are respectively, given by :
question: An electromagnetic wave of intensity $50 \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ enters in a medium of refractive index 'n' without any loss. The ratio of the magnitudes of electric fields, and the ratio of the magnitudes of magnetic fields of the wave before and after entering into the medium are respectively, given by :
solution:
The speed of electromagnetic wave in free space is given by

$\mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \epsilon_{0}}}$

In medium, $\mathbf{v}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{k} \in_{0} \mu_{0}}} \ldots$ (ii)

Dividing equation (i) by(ii), we get $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{V}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{n}$

$\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_{0} \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}=$ intensity $=\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_{0} \mathrm{kE}^{2} \mathrm{v}$

$\therefore \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{kE}^{2} \mathrm{v}$

$\Rightarrow \frac{E_{0}^{2}}{E^{2}}=\frac{k V}{C}=\frac{n^{2}}{n} \Rightarrow \frac{E_{0}}{E}=\sqrt{n}$

similarly

$\frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{C}}{2 \mu_{0}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{v}}{2 \mu_{0}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}}{\mathrm{~B}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{n}}}$

subject: Physics
q: If microwaves, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>‐</mo></math>rays, infrared, gamma rays, ultraviolet, radio waves and visible parts of the electromagnetic spectrum are denoted respectively by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> the following is the arrangement in ascending order of the wavelength
question: If microwaves, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>‐</mo></math>rays, infrared, gamma rays, ultraviolet, radio waves and visible parts of the electromagnetic spectrum are denoted respectively by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> the following is the arrangement in ascending order of the wavelength
solution: The electromagnetic (EM) spectrum is the range of all types of EM radiation. The γ‐ray has a maximum frequency or minimum wavelength; since the wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency, while radio wave has a minimum frequency and maximum wavelength. The order of wavelength will be; Gamma rays G < X-rays X < Ultra violet U < Visible rays V < Infrared rays I < Microwaves  M < Radio waves R.

subject: Physics
q: The energy associated with electric field is <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and with magnetic field is <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> for an electromagnetic wave in free space. Then:
question: The energy associated with electric field is <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and with magnetic field is <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>U</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> for an electromagnetic wave in free space. Then:
solution:
The average energy density of electric field is given by

uE=12εE02

Here, E0 is the maximum value of electric field.

The average energy density of magnetic field is given by

uB=B022μ

Here, B0 is the maximum value of magnetic field.

The speed of light is given by c2=1με

Also, E0=cB0

So, uE=12c2μcB02=B022μ=uB

As the energy density of electric field and magnetic field are equal, the energy associated for equal volume will also be equal.

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave of wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> has an intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>. It is propagating along the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>direction. The allowed expressions for the electric and magnetic fields are given by:
question: A plane electromagnetic wave of wavelength <math><mi>λ</mi></math> has an intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>. It is propagating along the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>direction. The allowed expressions for the electric and magnetic fields are given by:
solution: If <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is the amplitude of the electric field then the intensity of the electromagnetic wave is given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><msubsup><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn><mn>2</mn></msubsup><mo>×</mo><mi>c</mi></math>. From the above relation, we get <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>I</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>c</mi><mi> </mi><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math>. The relation between electric and magnetic fields is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mfrac><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>c</mi></mfrac></math>. The direction of <math> <mrow> <mover accent="true"> <mi>E</mi> <mo stretchy="true">→</mo> </mover> <mo>×</mo><mover accent="true"> <mi>B</mi> <mo stretchy="true">→</mo> </mover> </mrow> </math> will be along <math><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math>.

subject: Physics
q:
Identify the correct statements from the following descriptions of various properties of electromagnetic waves.

A. In a plane electromagnetic wave electric field and magnetic field must be perpendicular to each other and direction of propagation of wave should be along electric field or magnetic field.

B. The energy in electromagnetic wave is divided equally between electric and magnetic fields.

C. Both electric field and magnetic field are parallel to each other and perpendicular to the direction of propagation of wave.

D. The electric field, magnetic field and direction of propagation of wave must be perpendicular to each other.

E. The ratio of amplitude of magnetic field to the amplitude of electric field is equal to speed of light.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Identify the correct statements from the following descriptions of various properties of electromagnetic waves.

A. In a plane electromagnetic wave electric field and magnetic field must be perpendicular to each other and direction of propagation of wave should be along electric field or magnetic field.

B. The energy in electromagnetic wave is divided equally between electric and magnetic fields.

C. Both electric field and magnetic field are parallel to each other and perpendicular to the direction of propagation of wave.

D. The electric field, magnetic field and direction of propagation of wave must be perpendicular to each other.

E. The ratio of amplitude of magnetic field to the amplitude of electric field is equal to speed of light.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
In an electromagnetic wave electric field magnetic field and the direction of propagation of the wave are mutually perpendicular. Therefore, statement D is correct and statement A and C are incorrect.

The energy is equally divided between electric and magnetic field. Therefore, statement B is correct.

Speed of light c=E0B0. Therefore, statement E is incorrect.

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave travelling in $x$-direction is described by field equation $E_y = 300\sin\omega\left(t - \dfrac{x}{c}\right)$. If the electron is restricted to move in $y$-direction only with speed of $1.5 \times 10^6$ m/s then ratio of maximum electric and magnetic forces acting on the electron is _______.
question: An electromagnetic wave travelling in $x$-direction is described by field equation $E_y = 300\sin\omega\left(t - \dfrac{x}{c}\right)$. If the electron is restricted to move in $y$-direction only with speed of $1.5 \times 10^6$ m/s then ratio of maximum electric and magnetic forces acting on the electron is _______.
solution:
The maximum electric force acting on the electron is $F_e = eE_0$.

The maximum magnetic force acting on the electron is $F_m = evB_0$, where $v$ is the speed of the electron and $B_0$ is the maximum magnetic field.

The ratio of the maximum electric force to the maximum magnetic force is given by $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{eE_0}{evB_0} = \dfrac{E_0}{vB_0}$.

For an electromagnetic wave, the ratio of the amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields is $\dfrac{E_0}{B_0} = c$, where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum.

Substituting this into the ratio of forces yields $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{c}{v}$.

Given $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $v = 1.5 \times 10^6$ m/s, we get $\dfrac{F_e}{F_m} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 200$.

Answer: $200$

subject: Physics
q: Sun light falls normally on a surface of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and exerts an average force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> within a time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> minutes. Considering a case of complete absorption, the energy flux of incident light is
question: Sun light falls normally on a surface of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and exerts an average force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> within a time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> minutes. Considering a case of complete absorption, the energy flux of incident light is
solution:
The radiation pressure in case of complete absorption is given by,

P=Ic.

Therefore, the force will be,

F=Ic×A⇒I=FcA

⇒I=7.2×10-9×3×10836×10-4=600 W m-2⇒I=0.06 W cm-2

solution:
f=5×108 Hz

EM wave is travelling towards +j^

B→=8.0×10-8z^ T

E→=B→×C→=8×10-8z^×3×108y^

=-24x^  V m-1

q: An electron beam is accelerated by a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> to hit a metallic target to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. It produces continuous as well as characteristic <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></math> is the smallest possible wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>ray in the spectrum, the variation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> is correctly represented in :
question: An electron beam is accelerated by a potential difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> to hit a metallic target to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. It produces continuous as well as characteristic <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>rays. If <math><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></math> is the smallest possible wavelength of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>-</mo></math>ray in the spectrum, the variation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><msub><mi>λ</mi><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>log</mi><mfenced><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> is correctly represented in :
solution:
K.E.=eV,

KE=The kinetic energy of electron

The maximum energy of photon=K.E.

eV=hCλmin

⇒λmin V=hCe=constant

Taken log in both sides

lnλmin+lnV=lnconstant

⇒lnλmin=-lnV+ln(constant)

y=-ax+c the straight line of negative slope.
